speaker: interviewee
text: We moved in in this house in 1955. My husband had the hernia operation. He got sick, and they took him to Providence Hospital for an operation. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I-- the following, we lived a year, the following this was Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. I went to Young Israel Synagogue, [NON-ENGLISH] Israel was not here yet. And Rabbi Grushkin Synagogue was not here yet. So I went on college to Young Israel. And Nussbaum was the president, and I spoke to him. I say-- I spoke to him in Jewish. I said, I'm a newcomer. I'm here from 1949, and is holiday. And my husband, we getting just compensation, because he was sick, like, $46 a week. I would like to buy a ticket for Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. I'm not looking for, for nothing, but not to buy the full amount. Maybe you can give me for less. Because I'm used to since childhood, I used to go to-- especially the high holidays. And I talk to him. And he says to me, lady, if you can't afford to pay a ticket, go across the street, Lady Fatima Church and down there. That's what I started to cry. I should have told him that time. You know, so he sends me to across the street, on Coolidge and 10 Mile. He says, there is Lady Fatima Church. If you can't afford to buy a ticket, go there in church, and go pray there. So I started to cry, and I walked out. If I would have had the mind then, what I have now, I would have put it in the Jewish news, or I would have went to the rabbis or the president or whatever, and, and told them, oh, I should have told him off. You should have been ashamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the way you're treating the newcomers, that you're sending me here? I survived Hitler. I want to be a Jew. I want to remain after what they did to us, the German. And you sending me to Lady Fatima Church?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have told him off. But I didn't say a word. I shut my mouth. I didn't want to embarrass him, even he hurt me so much. And I cried like a baby. And I walked away. That what I wanted, you know, mention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that there's any of your experiences that happened to you during this time, do they still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Start snowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --impact you on a day-to-day basis?

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got to go start my car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it did a lot, a lot. When I sleep, for instance, I have lot of dreams, constantly dreaming. And it's a funny part. When I dream, they killing me. They, they, they-- I'm hiding in the ditches, and they coming after me. And they shooting me, they killing me. And I'm constantly alive. No matter how much they want to kill me, and I'm constantly alive. And at night, I scream and I shout. And I feel in my sleep that I'm screaming, shouting, up till now. Last night, I, I heard that, that, that they, they after me. They want to kill me. And this-- and, and no matter how much they want to catch me, kill me, and I'm always alive. The dreams are constantly bad dreams. And I'm constantly screaming and shouting, and I talking loud to myself. And I'm fighting. It affected a lot. It affected on my children a lot. When, when-- when he was young, before he went to work, he went in the morning. I don't want it, thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came home. He didn't see the children in a whole day. He should come from the factory to say, hello, how are you? How's the kids? After all, he didn't see us for eight, 10 hours a day. So right away-- I don't-- [INAUDIBLE]. So he worked very hard over there. And it was hard on him. So he came, he let it out on me, the screaming and the shouting, like it's my fault. And it's my-- because he took, my uncle took him to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I begged him. I was standing on my knees. Don't work by the uncle. Don't work, go someplace else. Go another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want out of my life? Don't work if you don't like it, they taking advantage of you. So he lets it out on me. And I let it  out on the children. And I always used to cry, why am alive, why am alive? I wish I'm dead. I'm going to suicide myself. So my daughter went to high school. And she lived there with fear. She was afraid. I'm coming home here, I don't know whether I should sit in high school and learn or I should come home and to see my mother is already dead, my mother suicide herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had no one to let it out. I made the biggest mistake I made. I should have cried to the walls. Because for my friends, I was afraid to, to, to complain and to cry. They knew anyways, my real life. But I didn't want it because you know, it's like gossiping.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I kept my mouth shut. And that was my mistake, that I had no business to, to beg, to be dead. And the children lived with fear. My mother is dying, I don't know if I'm going to find my mother home yet, alive. So it's affected them a lot, in a lot of ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I made a mistake. I made a lot of mistakes in, in my life. Important mistakes I made. Well, you can't take it back. It's too late now. And that's it. But the only thing I can say that, when I go-- up till now, when I go, for instance, to Hudson's department store and if I buy something, and I go to the sales lady, and I hear her German accent, I get so scared. I get so scared, so afraid, that seems to me she's going to come. And she's going to kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I take my clothes. I say, oh, no, no, I'm sorry. I have to look for something else. And I go to another sales lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't have the heart. I'm still, in me, shivering that she's going to do something for me because I'm Jewish and she's German. Even now, up till today, I'm still afraid. Or when I'm in a public among, you know, the American people and everything, and he talks to me Jewish and there's hardly any Jewish people here, if we go, for instance, 4th of July there's a you know, a concert or whatever, opera, and he speaks Jewish, I say, don't talk to me Jewish. Don't, how about speak to me English. Don't speak to me Jewish. Because there's not a lot Jewish people here. So the fear, it's still in my system. I'm still afraid. I'm still shivering that oy, oy, oy, you know. And the kids, they were so protected. And I was shivering when my son went, went outside to play. I was so oy, oy, oy, I don't see him. I'm going to tell you I used to run after him. And he was very unhappy, because the kids made fun out of him. Oh, mama's baby, mama's baby. You  know how the kids are. Already he was in public school. He was like seven, six, seven years old, eight years old. And I was afraid if he's still alive, if they didn't kill him. Or to go someplace here and there, and I was always afraid that they're going to kill him. They're going to be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So always-- in here, and he resented me for that. Because they made fun of him. They didn't want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and I used to take a glass of milk and run outside. Jay, look, here is a glass of milk. Jay, look, here's a banana. And he was already a grown up kid, you know, six, seven years old. It was a shame. You're hungry. You come in the house and have lunch. But that was in me and this. And he resented me for a long time for that. Ma, you ruined my life. And I told him-- I said, I'm sorry. I can't-- that was me. And I know I made mistakes. And I did the best I could. [SOBS] And the way I was to the children and the household and everything, I said, my mother didn't have a chance to teach me or to-- how and everything to run alive. Even after you're married, you go to  your mother. You ask her questions, they help you, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBS] I said, I didn't have anybody. So I did the best I could. And this is it, so what can I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I apologize. Forgive me. I didn't do mistakes. Like, I was not a good mother and everything. I say, [SOBBING] it hurts a lot of things that I can't talk. Because it hurts. That's it, let's make it the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, let's pause here.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures was taken in, in 1937, in 1938. On the left side is my older sister. Her name is Dinka [NON-ENGLISH] because she's from the first mother's marriage. And on my right is Yentl. She's named after my grandfather, after my father's father. And Yentl Kukes. Those are my two sisters. And they were perished in the ghetto in 1942 in Berezne. Those are the two matzevahs that was taken in Berezne, where it was the slaughterhouse, the two big graves. And it was written in Hebrew, and it was written in Russia. And, and it's buried there, like, 2,000, 2,300 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people that-- the few people that they survived Hitler, they survived the war, they came to Berezne at the cemetery. And they put those, those matzevahs. And when I was in Israel in 1968, I met some friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me a copy from those matzevahs, that they were there. And after the war, they went to Israel. So that's what I have, the pictures. This is the third matzevah. They had three matzevahs made, three graves, that, all together was 3,200 Jew was killed in 1942. And those are the people that they left over. And my cousin, it's to the left, the third from the matzevah. His name is Herschel Czechek, my first cousin. And that he survived. His wife, with, with two children were killed. And they gave it to me, for, for remembrance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was, in 1968, is Israel. He lived in Israel. But now he, he passed away. He is now there [NON-ENGLISH]. He was born in Klasov, with whole family. My mother comes from the, the whole family comes from Klasov. But the father was in Berezne. And so we lived here, and they were not far away from us. And the rest of the people, they are from Klasov. They are from Berezne, they are for-- all around. Let's say like state Michigan, they are like the state of Berezne. The, the Klasov people, they took them all around, and they were in Berezne ghetto. And those are the three big matzevahs that they were buried. And they were there too from small villages and from small [NON-ENGLISH] and from small cities. And they all together. This is my-- this is the picture that my younger daughter got married. And I am in the middle, in the pink dress, with my husband. And on the left, it's my son-in-law and my older daughter, Ray Tremblay and Goldie. And that's her son. He's-- he was about four or five years old. And my husband and I. And this is my younger daughter. And that's her husband, Rodney Harwood. And that's my son, Jay Cimmer, on the end, on the right-hand side. They were married in 1982. This is my grandson. He's 20 years old, now in Michigan State College. He's a wonderful kid. He's very, very kind to me, very good to me when he comes from Michigan State. He comes to visit me. Every year, I'm having the children in the Hanukkah party. We light candles, we sing Hanukkah songs. I gave them nice gifts. And he loves me dearly, and I love him too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jay Tremblay-- Daniel, Daniel. He is named after my sister, Tremblay. This is a picture from, it's, from my older daughter, her daughter. It's my granddaughter, Shoshanna Tremblay. She's going to be 16 years old in April 21. She's a lovely girl. She always calls me, Bubbe, I love you. Bubbe, I love your gefilte fish. Bubbe, I, make me some chicken soup. I love it and Bubbe, teach me how to make gefilte fish. And when we make a Hanukkah party, I can show you the, the, the letters, they come, and how she writes to me. If you, if you remind, it's over there. That she writes, Bubbe, thank you. I love you dearly. and I appreciate-- even if you gave every time, I gave her a few dollars, $5, $10. And, Bubbe, even you giving me the money or not, I love you just as much dearly without that you're helping me. This is my younger daughter, her son. He is the oldest, Benjamin Harwood. Now he's-- in January the 7th it's his birthday. Last year was his bar mitzvah. He had a wonderful bar mitzvah. The went to Israel. And they made in Masada. And he's a, a excellent kid, very, very friendly. Every time he comes, he hugs me. He kisses me. Bubbe, I love you. He loves to sleep here. He loves to play Rummikub with me. He loves to play cards with me. And he's smarter than I am. He always wins. And in shul, they belong in Adat Shalom Synagogue. And he's a brilliant kid. The rabbis and the teachers, they love him dearly. And I love him too. This is, my younger daughter, her daughter, my granddaughter, Brittany Harwood. She's going to be-- she's going to be 10 years old in February the 9th, it's her birthday. And she's a lovely girl. She's very smart. And we're very close to each other. She loves to come here. She loves to bake. I let her bake cookies, chocolate chip cookies. I let her [NON-ENGLISH] with the doll. And she plays Rummikub with me. And she loves to sleep with me in bed together, and she hugs me, and I love her dearly. This is my immediate family. This is my husband, Albert Cimmer, my granddaughter, Janna Tremblay, my older daughter Goldie Tremblay, my, my son-in-law, Ray Tremblay. And I adore them very much, and I appreciate that they came. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: have another grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they take part in my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel, Daniel. Tell them about Daniel, that I have another grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm happy that I survive. Like you said, I, you know, I have a lovely four grandchildren and a lovely daughters and my, and my son-laws. They're very kind to me, they're very good to me. When I see them, I keep forgetting what-- the hell what I went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have another daughter to tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I would like to, to live longer, to see to marry off my grandchildren and to take part in their weddings and my son too. And they love me, and I love them. I appreciate very, very well that they are here. And they're very good to me, especially my grandson and my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell them from the second daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got another daughter, tell her. We've got another daughter and two grandchildren, tell her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have another daughter with a son-in-law and two grandchildren, which they couldn't make it today. And my son is in Connecticut. He couldn't make it today. He's the one, and my daughter, both of them. They, they were working hard to-- I should, I should take-- should come this moment and to, to, to make all the biography about my life, about my survival, which now I appreciate.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got another grandchild, Daniel. You forgot him. He's going to be a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel, he's in school. He's going to be a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you are not allowed. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I have a sister in New York. And she have six grandchildren. And we doing all right, or I hope everything will be all right. I don't know. We was last year, we went to bat mitzvah. They took us eight hours to sit up an airplane. [LAUGHTER] And I was sitting with this one here. And we was thinking we don't make it. Although thank god, we've done it. You know, it was so bad to drive. You know, it was bad on an airplane.

words.text: it was bad on an airplane.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Albert, that's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, now, now that take us-- they take us-- we, we, we pray in buying some tickets. We praying to buy some tickets. [LAUGHTER] It's a couple million dollars to take care of my grandchildren. That's why I'm praying.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I told you. He's worse than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the conclusion of the videotaping session. We used six tapes.
